title: Proxy vs Load Balancer
description:
## Pick the Right Component

You run **three identical backend application servers** and need a component sitting in front of them whose job is to **spread incoming client requests evenly across all three** for scale and availability.

Which component is the best fit for this specific job?

options.text: A **forward proxy** — clients route their outbound traffic through it to reach the internet.
A **load balancer** — it distributes incoming requests across the pool of identical backends.
A **reverse proxy** acting purely as a single static-file web server.
A **firewall** — it filters traffic by rules but does not distribute it.

explanation: Distributing incoming requests across a pool of identical backends is precisely the job of a **load balancer**. A **forward proxy** (a) faces the other way — it acts on behalf of *clients* sending their outbound traffic to the internet. A **reverse proxy** (c) does face the backends and can terminate TLS and route by path/host, and in practice many reverse proxies *also* load balance — but a reverse proxy whose role is just static serving doesn't distribute load. A **firewall** (d) filters traffic; it doesn't balance it. (Rule of thumb: *distribute traffic across identical servers* → load balancer; *terminate TLS and route by URL path in front of backends* → reverse proxy; *clients route outbound through it* → forward proxy.)

hints.title: Match the verb to the tool
hints.body: The keyword here is 'distribute across identical backends'. One component is named for exactly that job.
